step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("definition of lead-time bias in cancer screening and how it affects survival metrics")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved evidence, I confirm that lead-time bias specifically describes the artificial extension of survival time seen when diseases like GBM are detected earlier via screening—even though the biological behavior and ultimate outcome (mortality) remain unchanged. The retrieved literature explicitly states that lead-time bias leads to inflated survival estimates because diagnoses occur earlier, shifting the timeline without affecting actual disease progression rates. While length-time bias relates to detecting slower-growing, indolent tumors rather than fast-progressing ones—which could skew results differently—it applies here only if the asymptomatic cohort contained significantly less aggressive forms of GBM. But since the question specifies equal progression despite earlier detection, and notes identical post-diagnostic trajectories, this confirms pure lead-time bias—not selection bias or observer expectations. Therefore, my prior agreement stands.
\boxed{D}
Note: No additional justification needed beyond confirming alignment with definition and application from evidence. Final answer matches previously held view. We do not introduce new reasons unless necessary; here, existing evidence fully validates D. Disagreement is unnecessary. All sources consistently define lead-time bias as exactly the scenario presented: earlier diagnosis → longer measured survival → no real difference in mortality. Thus, D is correct.
Final Answer: \boxed{D}
